desc:
A warmth fills your heart as you enter a spacious room dimly lit by torches in
the four corners of the room. Quickly catching your attention in the centre is a
large marble statue glowing faintly. The statue is Sylan's noble father, a
soldier of the Arthedain. His face appears firm and solemn, ready to engrave
himself in battle at any cost to protect the city of Arthedain. Among the walls
are many tapestries filled with beautiful art and paintings showing the
renovation of the new city of Fornost. The crest of the Arthedain is mounted on
the wall across from you with battle insignias and jewels surrounding it. To the
east is another lit room.
name: The Foyer

desc:
This vast area appears to be Sylan's hall of honor. His nobleness and dedication
to the Knights of Arthedain is evidently reflected throughout this room. Along
the floor are long carpets of battle insignias foreign to you. Written in
Westron is a scripture mounted under another crest of Arthedain. Looking at it
closely you see the following: "For I have taken the Arthedain solemn promise to
protect the cities of Fornost and Bree from evil at any cost to my life. I will
uphold and protect the walls of Norbury my family has worked in vain to build.
--- Sylan, Noble Knight of Arthedain. At the top of the hall is a window which
almost pours out the vastness of the sky, allowing the sun to give warmth to
your bones and the moon to shine it's glimmering rays among the many trophies of
battles and challenges Sylan has won. Thick metal and chain mail armour hangs on
the northern wall along with an empty sheath in the middle. The size and
vastness of the sheath makes it evident that the sword of the kingdom of
Arthedain is not one to reckon with. To the west is the foyer and the east
Sylan's quarters.
name: Hall of Trophies

desc:
This room stands in stark comparison to the rest of this elegant home. The walls
are spartan and free of decoration -- save a few candles along the walls and a
large oak desk along the northern wall. A pile of furs lay in a corner near a
small lantern, clearly, where Sylan prefers to sleep.
name: Sylan's Quarters

desc:
As a strong contrast to the majestic hall of statues just south of here. This is
a cosy and small livingroom, which seems perfect for a dwarf in need of rest.
The ceiling if far lower than the hall and is made of oak instead of stone. The
rich wood gives the room a deep luxurious aura. The floor is covered with a
colourful rag carpet which looks a bit misplaced here, perhaps it was a gift
from a friend. A well-used oil lamp can be seen on a black bedside table and the
bed itself stands along a short side of the room.
name: Astroc's Bedroom

desc:
Before the Kingdom of Arthedain fell to the raid of the witch-kings, Arnor Lane
was one of the noblest areas of Fornost. Here, a few great ruins are waiting to
be rebuilt: their current shape still shows traces of their former glamour. The
coat-of-arms of the Noirins-family is still visible in the old pavement. Many
new houses have been built between the old ruins, ruins that appears to have
been used as quarries.
name: Arnor Lane

desc:
Arnor Street ends in front of a magnificent ruin which might once have been a
guild or court hall. Most of the other ruins have already been used as quarry to
get new stones for all the newer buildings that can be seen around. The old hall
seems to have become some sort of small park, as the roof has collapsed long ago
and is overgrown with grasses and mosses by now. Other plants, even trees, were
also able to take root. The coat-of-arms of the Tarma family, inlaid in the
pavement of the road, is one of the few still intact leftovers of a time that is
long gone.
name: The End of Arnor Lane

desc:
This room is floored and lined with polished white marble, furrowed with black
veins of sparkling ore. The ceiling is a great dome, decorated with frescoes of
great battles and myths of Middle-earth's past, and the floor bears the mark of
a giant elf-rune. The south wall is broken by a large window, framed with red
curtains which can be flung back to let in the sunlight. An open archway leads
north to what appears to be a sorcerous work-room, and a pair of great doors
with lions carved into the woodwork lead east.
name: A Domed Chamber

desc:
Flasks and alembics cover a massive oak work table. On one side of the work
table are clustered piles of books and scrolls. A cage with a small ferret
inside hangs from the ceiling, and hung from the east wall is a great tapestry
upon which has been painted a detailed map of Middle-earth.
name: A Cluttered Studyroom

desc:
A great canopy-bed hung with golden curtains dominates the room. To one side is
a polished oak armoire, and beside a fancifully carved writing desk. On the
opposite side of the room is a fireplace that burns without apparent need to be
refueled. Hung on the walls are trophies of war: orc scimitars and shields.
name: A Cozy Bedchamber

desc:
The Arnor Lane passes a small park that houses the ruins of an once palace-like
building. All other ruins in this short section of the road were removed and
have been replaced with new houses. In one place, the coat-of-arms of the
Emeries family, one of the seven major noble houses of the fallen Kingdom of
Arthedain, has been inlaid into the roadway. The colourful stones are covered
with dirt and are only partly visible. A wild park can be seen towards the
south.
name: Arnor Lane

desc:
Here, Arnor Lane bends in a wide curve forming a right angle around the ruin of
an odeon or theatre. Bushes and even a few small trees have taken root in and
around that fallen building, while a few smaller new houses have been built near
it. The now-shattered coat-of-arms of the Foros family is inlaid in the pavement
in the road, right in front of the broken theatre.
name: Arnor Lane

desc:
This comfortable-looking villa gives a serene and cosy impression. There are a
few bedrooms, a living room and a kitchen. All the rooms have large windows,
allowing sunlight to enlighten the house round the day. The furniture has been
made of light-coloured wood. Flowers and herbs are growing all around the house,
adding a sweet, fresh scent to the air. In front of the house there is a
well-kept, large patio that opens up towards the Arnor Lane.
name: Oriza's Villa

desc:
This small house has been built entirely from wood. The construction work is of
high quality, and the room gives a light and cosy impression. An eloquent
picture has been hung on the wall, portraying a young Dunadan woman. The other
furniture of the house consist of some cupboards, a table with a set of chairs
around it, and of a soft-looking bed in the corner. The floor is covered with a
wide, forest-green carpet. There are numerous flower pots on the window sills,
and outside one can see a ruined theatre.
name: Sari's House

desc:
A proud garden spreads out here between the buildings. It is defying the
surroundings with its tamed, disciplined flora and serves as an invitation to
the low white round tower to the north. A richly adorned white marble fountain
makes out the heart and centre of the garden. Broad waves of flowers roll out in
growing circles from it, the perfect shape interrupted only by narrow walkways
leading to a small adjacent stone building and the entrance to the tower in the
north.
name: A Secluded Garden

desc:
The stone floor has recently been thoroughly broomed and is, despite the bare
earth showing in the gaps, clean and tidy. The building, consisting of one big
room, has only three walls to allow horses to easily enter from the west. In the
middle of the house, a four foot high wooden plank divides the space in two. On
the walls of the southern part hang bridles and saddles together with horse
shoes and grooming brushes. Next to the plank stands a large water trough that
spans almost the whole breadth of the room. Securely fastened to the north wall
is a massive workbench, filled with various tools and gardening utensils.
name: A Simple Building at the Garden's Edge

desc:
Deeply stained wood panels line the floor, walls, and ceiling of this large
hall. There are finely crafted tapestries depicting ancient battles lining the
walls. A tinted glass lined cabinet stands against the outer wall. Figurines of
unique and exotic creatures, made of various precious metals, line the cabinets'
shelves. The figurines stand as a visual testament to foes slain.
name: An Ornate Entrance Hall

desc:
A large window opens up in the ceiling, allowing sunlight to reflect from the
mirrors set up on the walls, efficiently lighting up the den on sunny days. A
weapon rack filled with a collection of weapons and memorabilia, likely gathered
during the adventures of the houseowner, is set up on one of the walls. On the
other side of the room stands a sturdy bed, next to a table cluttered with maps
covering every nook of Arda. A grand chair filled with inscribed notes of vile
creatures slain by Razputin, sits in the middle of the room. It faces a wall
with portrays of the entire bloodline of this mighty dwarf.
name: Razputin's Den

desc:
There are no walls separating the different sections of this floor. Instead it
all molds into a pleasant blend of comfort and function. An iron stove stands
against the wall in the northwest corner of the tower, not far from the simple
yet elegant dining table, large enough for several guests. On the opposing side
stands a row of bookshelves, skillfully crafted to fit the curve of the wall.
Between the stove and the two armchairs resting on a red carpet below the
shelves, an open fireplace provides some light to the windowless room. A large
spiral staircase leads up to the second floor of the tower.
name: The Bottom Floor of the Tower

desc:
The room is crafted out of a thousand dark grey rectangle stones and styled as
if it were a giant keep. Several tapestries are lined up on most walls, save for
the northern wall where a map hangs instead. A few torches take up the rest of
the empty space. A few end tables bear random trinkets and a few chairs are
lined with a variety of elaborate cloaks for mending. It feels like home.
name: Radar's Keep

desc:
All around are the remains of old ruins. Many plants, from grass to bushes and
even trees, have been able to take root in between the broken buildings. The
result is a wild park right in the city of fornost. Long vines and other
creeping plants cover many of the old stones. It appears that some citizens have
built small houses in places at the borders of the wild park.
name: Arnor Park

desc:
Rustic stones reach high around a marble door, with opulent tapestries lining
each wall of the entranceway. In stark contrast is the ceiling overhead,
depicting several identical white trees. The entrance also serves as an
impromptu cloakroom, with several coat racks standing near the door. A solid
purple carpet with gold-coloured fringes provides a soft surface to walk upon.
name: Front Entrance of a Tower

desc:
Two sets of carpeted stairs occupy much of the nexus, leading up and down in a
clock-wise direction. A wavy oaken bannister follows the stairs, offering a
handhold for unsteady feet. Two rectangular metal hatches are installed on
either side of the stairs. The left hatch is marked as a chute, while the right
hatch is accompanied by securely fastened ropes, branding it a dumbwaiter. A
small plaque has been fastened on one of the walls.
name: Staircase

desc:
A carpeted hallway passes by many doors of various makes and sizes, abodes of
the locals of Fornost. Small lanterns emit a muted light and a musty smell
permeates the air, both due to a lack of windows. A few tapestries decorate the
walls, although many of them have a small coat of dust upon them. Well-cut
stones lie behind and beside the tapestries, a sign of a robust and durable
building.
name: Hallway

desc:
Several small lanterns have been fastened against the walls of the hallway,
adding a touch of light to illuminate the way, but not enough to notice the dust
that has accumulated. A carpet that is mostly violet with yellow-coloured
fringes lines the hallway in a north-south fashion, passing by a multitude of
different doors, each a different size and make. A few small paintings have been
hung on the walls, yet care has been taken that they are far away from the
lanterns.
name: Hallway

desc:
A luxurious purple carpet with frayed fringes covers most of the hallway floor,
passing by several small apartments. The doors that lead into these dwellings
vary in size and make, some locked and others bolted shut. The overhead ceiling
is only eight feet high, tall enough for most Dunedain. A few lanterns are
ensconced along the wall, providing light where the Sun cannot reach.
name: Hallway

desc:
A small layer of dust is visible by the light of small lanterns that have been
attached to the walls in between some of the doors that line the hallway. A
slightly frayed purple carpet with yellow fringes covers most of the floor,
following the hallway to both the east and the south. No tapestries or paintings
can be found in this section, although the wall on the eastern side features a
large mural.
name: Hallway

desc:
Small lanterns set in the walls illuminate the carpeted hallway, providing a
small amount of heat and light to lead visitors of the tower to their
destination. The rest of the walls are unusually bare, although some of the
stones have been etched with seemingly random symbols. The ceiling overhead is
equally bare, but a warm violet carpet with gold fringes covers much of the
floor. The northeast corner is anything but bare, and seems to be a haven for
clutter.
name: Hallway

desc:
A few bookshelves line the eastern wall, mostly devoid of books and somewhat
dusty. The books that are present are arranged chaotically and bear signs of
misuse. A few writing desks with matching chairs form somewhat of a circle in
the middle of the room. The northern corner bears a small window with a cage
hanging next to it. Small feathers and down are sprinkled in and below the cage,
as well as a specks of parchment and a single broken small tube.
name: Reading Room

desc:
A staircase occupies most of the area, with the stairs going both up and down in
a clockwise fashion. A blockage of several wooden planks nailed together has
been erected to the south, preventing any from exploring this floor. At the
moment, the only way out would be to go up or down the stairs. A wavy bannister
made of oak serves as a handrail.
name: Staircase

desc:
The stairs and wavy oak bannister end at the top of the tower. A balcony can be
spotted just to the south. Three small rooms are marked in the other directions,
their doors elaborate with lush tapestries hanging next to them. Two large
lanterns have been affixed to the walls, lighting up the entire staircase and
small hallway.
name: Top of the Tower

desc:
A wavy oaken bannister ends in a swirl as the stairs it accompanies reaches the
bottom. The end of a chute can be seen on the western wall, its shaft poking out
of the wall as no hatch contains it. On the floor below the chute, several small
fragments of bark remain, insinuating a recent use for moving firewood. A
rectangular hatch can be found on the eastern side, labelled as a dumb waiter.
name: Bottom of a Staircase

desc:
A stone floor leads out to the outside, offering a good view over the city of
Fornost. A balcony has been created out of several oak planks. An oaken
balustrade serves as a railing, its spindles shaped like long Dunedain blades. A
few planters stand on either side of the balcony, although they are devoid of
any sort of vegetation. The balcony is barely sheltered, thus anyone upon it
must suffer the rigours of the weather.
name: Tower Balcony

desc:
Roughly dug dirt walls are reinforced by wooden support beams, creating a five
foot high tunnel. The beams are quite sturdy and well maintained. A few lanterns
have been securely attached with metal rods, their light showing a set of doors
of varying sizes lining the walls. The packed dirt floor is mostly devoid of
debris, although some dirt has moved around a bit due to trampling boots,
collecting on a thick mat that ends at a stone entrance to the north. The tunnel
continues to both the east and the west.
name: Excavated Tunnel

desc:
A five foot high tunnel, dug out of thoroughly serves as a hallway for
underground dwellings. Several small rough apartments have been created out of
coarse dirt walls and wooden support beams, each exterior built with their own
distinct flair. A lantern accompanies every door, so visitors and inhabitants
alike can find their destination with ease. Some small rugs cover the packed
dirt floor, where travellers can stomp the dirt off their boots. The tunnel
continues to the west, where more homes can be found.
name: Excavated Tunnel

desc:
A small stack of firewood, securely bound, stands neatly piled up against the
western wall. A few empty boxes, odd pieces of furniture and a small broom
closet make up the rest for the storage room. Near the closet is a small pile of
dust, small wood chips and dust web: the sweepings from the basement area. One
lamp attached on one of the walls, almost at the ceiling, lights up most of the
storage room.
name: Storage Room

desc:
Several wine racks line the northern wall, yet they are devoid of any actual
wine, hinting of either a rambunctious night or a poor supply. The fruity smell
of wine does linger, coming from several empty wine bottles that lie in a small
pile beside a rack. The wine cellar is quite humid and no light sources adorn
its walls, in order to protect the vintage. Round streaks mar the ground, an
indication that several barrels were once stored here.
name: Wine Cellar

desc:
The floor of this large room is elegantly set in a circular pattern, resembling
the garden outside. Rings of marble in different nuances decorate the floor like
ripples on a calm pool, spreading out from the spiralling iron staircase that
runs through the centre of the whole tower like a giant metal spine. Large
lancet windows distributed symmetrically around the walls allow the sunlight to
shine in freely, bathing the entire room in bright light. Between the windows
hang large silver chandeliers together with tapestries, showing images of proud
warriors in battle. Two gilded beds stand on either side of the chamber.
name: Inside the White Tower

desc:
This sparsely furnitured room, albeit extremely messy to the point where it
almost seems to be abandoned, somehow manages to give a homey impression.
Various items such as magic scrolls, poorly written in Khuzdul and beer mugs
have been carelessly left on the rocky floor. Dim light is shining in through a
dirty lantern that is hanging from the ceiling. In the middle of the room there
is a giant oak table with a small brewery that wouldn't even meet the standards
of Olly's Tavern. A faint odour of yeast seems to be permanent in here.
name: Brewer's Brewery

desc:
As you enter this home, the smell of sweat and leather fill your nose. Lying
around the western corner are various types of dwarven training equipment which
are all well used. You notice an office in the northern corner, whereupon a
picture of Khurmin and his dwarven family is placed. In the eastern corner lies
a makeshift bed, comfortable for most dwarves. Looking upwards you notice
frescoes depicting the many wars of Arda.
name: Dwarven Training Home

desc:
Shelves dominate three of the walls here, with a bed standing in the corner of
the fourth wall. A wooden table and chairs stand in the middle of the room,
across from the door. The door appears to be plated with a darkened steel, which
provides more protection to the shelter. The stone walls look carefully
constructed and look sturdy enough to withstand anything. In each corner of the
ceiling there are small lanterns attached to steel hooks, bolted firmly into the
wall at the top; the soft light emitting from the silver lanterns create a calm
ambience to the bunker, yet provide enough light to see.
name: Dawnborn's Shelter

desc:
Some clean, fresh straw covers the floor of this stable. A few stalls line one
end, each crafted from solid oak. The smell of feed mixed with a faint whiff of
manure fills the area. On the northern end, a solid cellar door with an iron
lock has been set into the ground.
name: Well-kept Stable

desc:
Thick slabs of stone make up the walls of this chamber, obviously brought here
at great expense from some western mountain. The stone is rough, covered in
sharp protrusions and pocked depressions.
name: Stone-lined Chamber

desc:
Massive oak doors, iron-banded and crested, open to a hall carved from black
slate. Flickering torches illuminate ceiling frescoes while statues of heroes
framed by stone columns watch silently from the room's edges. Against one wall,
an ancient tapestry hangs loosely. Depicting the historic siege of Fornost, its
once-bright colours have long dulled, and its edges frayed. The dark stone
floor, polished to a mirror finish, leads to an intricately carved staircase
that ascends into the heart of the keep.
name: Ancient Antechamber

desc:
The sheer stonework of the tower opens to expose this broad terrace to the sky.
Numerous chairs and sofas are arrayed across the space, many under simple covers
that offer some protection from the sun and weather. The balcony, bounded by
ornamental stone railings, provides a stellar view of the northern edge of the
city of Fornost, and of the bleak wilderness to the north, ever shadowed by the
past horrors of Angmar.
name: Broad Stone Terrace

desc:
The walls are lined with large, detailed maps pinned to wooden boards,
showcasing various regions and the battlefronts of Arda. A massive oak table
occupies the centre of the room, its surface covered with miniature soldiers,
tactical charts, and scrolls, indicative of ongoing strategic planning. Flanking
this table are three high-backed chairs of dark leather, bearing the marks of
numerous councils. The room is anchored by a large, ornate fireplace that
provides warmth and focus. The polished stone walls are decorated with banners
bearing the sigils of Numenor and the Dunedain, while polished armour stands
display ceremonial and battle gear. At the far end of the room, to the left of
the fireplace, a closet can be seen filled with the trophies taken as spoils of
war. The air is redolent with the scent of parchment and ink, sweat and musk -
all underscoring the weighty decisions made within these walls. To the north
lies a scarcely discernible doorway, concealed behind an exquisite carving of
Nimloth, the White Tree of Numenor.
name: Royal War Room

desc:
The office of Telemmaite, a space of solemn authority and contemplative wisdom,
appears untouched by time. Dominated by a large, intricately carved oak desk,
the room conveys an air of dignified purpose. Upon the desk is a smattering of
mithril artefacts gathered from all corners of Arda, while maps and plans are
meticulously arranged and held in place with large mithril paperweights. Behind
the desk, a towering throne, freshly upholstered in dark forest-green leather
and stitched with mithril, commands respect. Shelves line the walls, filled to
the brim with ancient tomes, scrolls, and Numenorean artifacts. A grand window,
framed with heavy velvet drapes, offers a sweeping view of the North Downs, for
providing both inspiration as well as a backdrop for contemplation. High-backed
chairs upholstered in rich fabrics are arranged around a low table in the corner
of the room, covered in reports of the Orc and Black Numenorean revival.
name: Office of Telemmaite

desc:
The room hosts a wide, open layout, save for a few screens that obscure a simple
bed and cabinet. The centre of the room hosts a heavy wooden table, upon which
scores of letters, detailed inventories, and other paperwork are chaotically
arranged. The dark-paneled walls of the room are lined with display shelves,
containing a range of weapons, relics, and other curios. A broad canvas
clarifies the source of the collection: a map of Forochel, highlighting the fell
Icebay, the edges of the Iron Mountains, and vague borders of the fallen horrors
of Angmar.
name: Office of Sirris
